currentUse: Buddhist temple — the "Five World Buddhas Temple" of Guang Huan Mi Zong / World Peace and Health Organization, 262 E. Main St., Amsterdam, NY
summary: St. Casimir's, the Lithuanian parish of Amsterdam, NY, was suppressed by the Diocese of Albany in 2009, and the building on East Main Street was sold within about a year to a Chinese Buddhist movement led by Lucas Wang ("Holy Master Ziguang Shang Shi"), whose World Peace and Health Organization converted it into the Five World Buddhas Temple, formally dedicated October 27, 2010. The group's own literature says it "carefully restored the abandoned and decaying structure," and the sanctuary today mixes Buddhist statuary and tapestries with the church's original Christian stained glass and murals. The St. Casimir statue with its Lithuanian inscription remains on the tower — the most visible surviving marker of the parish's Lithuanian identity — while Lithuanian memorabilia collected by the parish's priests was relocated to Amsterdam's Walter Elwood Museum. The purchase was part of a much larger 2010 buying spree in which the WPHO paid over $3.5 million in cash for two closed Catholic churches (St. Casimir's and St. Michael's), a former elementary school, more than 40 foreclosed homes, and a summer camp, with announced ambitions of a $1 billion spiritual-tourism development including a theme park. The organization remains active in the Amsterdam area, though the temple has historically not been open to outsiders. The building stands; the parish community does not — there is no evidence of any Lithuanian congregational activity, reunions, or organized preservation effort attached to the site.

developments.headline: Parish suppressed by the Diocese of Albany
developments.detail: St. Casimir's of Amsterdam — identified as the city's Lithuanian parish — appears on the diocese's list of suppressed parishes with a 2009 closure, part of the Albany diocese's 'Called to Be Church' consolidation wave. A March 2010 report indicated the building was still on the market shortly after closure.

sources.title: List of suppressed parishes in the Roman Catholic Diocese of Albany

developments.headline: Sold to Buddhist group amid a $3.5M Amsterdam property spree
developments.detail: Beginning June 2010, the World Peace and Health Organization, led by Ziguang Shang Shi (Lucas Wang), paid over $3.5 million in cash for two closed Catholic churches, a former elementary school, over 40 foreclosed homes, and a large summer camp in and around Amsterdam, announcing plans for a spiritual and economic 'renaissance' including a solar plant, cemetery, cultural exchange center, and theme park with a promised $1 billion investment. St. Casimir's price individually was not published in sources reviewed.

sources.title: Buddhists Promise a Renaissance for Amsterdam, New York

developments.headline: Dedicated as the Five Buddhas Temple
developments.detail: The former St. Casimir's was formally dedicated as the Five Buddhas Temple on October 27, 2010, with five Buddha statues imported from China unveiled. The movement's own site (Guang Huan Mi Zong) says the temple was established in 2010, that the group 'carefully restored the abandoned and decaying structure,' and that the main hall today holds Buddhist statues and tapestries alongside the original Christian stained glass and murals, hosting Health Dharma classes and Buddhist ceremonies.

sources.title: Buddhists Promise a Renaissance for Amsterdam, New York

developments.headline: Lithuanian traces: tower statue survives, memorabilia moved to museum
developments.detail: Per the Lithuanian heritage survey Global True Lithuania, the St. Casimir statue with a Lithuanian inscription remains on the tower; some sources report the stained glass windows remain inside, and a 2018 commenter reported murals and ceiling decorations intact. The temple typically does not admit outsiders. Lithuanian memorabilia collected by the parish's priests was relocated to the Walter Elwood Museum of Amsterdam history.

sources.title: Upstate New York: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions. The archive record ("closed ~May 2009, Diocese of Albany; building intact as of 2019") is confirmed and materially refined: the building did not merely sit intact — it was sold in 2010, roughly a year after closure, to the World Peace and Health Organization and converted into the Five World Buddhas Temple, dedicated October 27, 2010. The Wikipedia suppressed-parishes list independently confirms 2009 closure and identifies the parish as Lithuanian, matching the archive. Note one nuance: the Buddhist group's own page describes St. Casimir as a "Polish Prince" — a hagiographic point about the saint, not evidence against the parish's Lithuanian identity, which Global True Lithuania and Wikipedia both confirm for the Amsterdam parish.
gaps: The individual sale price for St. Casimir's was not published in any source I could open — only the aggregate figure (WPHO paid over $3.5M for two churches, a school, 40+ homes, and a camp in 2010). Exact sale date/deed details unverified (county deed records not searched). Current (2026) day-to-day operational status of the temple at 262 E. Main St. specifically is unverified: the Guang Huan Mi Zong organization remains active in the Amsterdam area (search results surfaced 2025-2026 events, including a May 2026 Buddha's Birthday celebration, but at 2337 NY-5S, their other Amsterdam-area site), and I could not directly confirm the E. Main St. building's present condition or use. Daily Gazette coverage that would add texture — "Buddhists planning to leave Amsterdam" (2011), "What went wrong between Buddhist group and Amsterdam?" (2015), "World Peace and Health group still sold on Amsterdam" (2018) — returned HTTP 429 on every fetch attempt, so nothing from those articles is used here. No evidence found of Lithuanian community reunions, preservation campaigns, or diocesan announcements concerning the site; whether the Walter Elwood Museum (which suffered flood damage in 2011, unverified here) still holds the Lithuanian memorabilia was not confirmed.
